and then you're not getting a speed up there.
And then PyTorch has its own requirements.
PyTorch doesn't have int4.
A lot of modules don't support quantization at all
in PyTorch, like something like an RNN,
which is now becoming popular again.
It's not really supporting quantization right now.
So you definitely, if you're trying
to go this route for a practical application,
you'll want to know what your hardware is,
what your framework is, and what you can actually support
with the ways you want to compress to your modules.
And one last thing I'll say is that both of the methods
I showed so far, they both have to,
they have their own customized hardware accelerators
that they wrote to make those things work.
And this is like a lot of work,
and most people probably don't have the time to do this.
So there are methods that I haven't shown here
that do quantization in a way that is effective
without having to rewrite your framework
or your hardware accelerator.
But this is definitely something to consider with quantization.
OK, so now I think I've motivated
why post-training quantization is hard,
because you're throwing away precision, which
can make it hard to get the most out of the network
that you have trained.
So attempting IDEA here is now, let's say we know
we're going to quantize our model.
Let's train the model with quantization in mind.
And now we can revisit the example I
showed before of binarized neural networks, which didn't work.
But it actually can work if you train with the binarization
in mind.
So a paper in 2016, they consider the case
where all of your weights were negative 1 or 1.
Activations were also negative 1 or 1.
And they do some clever statistics to make that work.
And then the gradients that you back propagate through the model
are also negative.
They're also discrete.
And so it's probably kind of surprising that this works.
But they basically are using the core mechanisms
that we use to train neural networks
and using fancy statistics to make this work for binary values.
And by doing this, they get kind of shockingly good results.
Like on C-FAR 10, which was a very popular image classification
data set for a while, they get a 10% test set error.
And at the time, like a state of the art method
was at a little under 12%.
So they're basically matching or beating
some of these extremely strong models at that time.
And they used effectively the same architecture
from some of these models, but just binarized.
So this was sort of a proof of concept
that if you quantize during training,
you can match performance and get a much smaller model,
which I think was a really surprising finding.
And then a more recent work that I think is really cool
is that for doing quantization,
one of the things you can do is you
can start with your model that is full precision, not quantized.
And then you can basically train each layer, one layer at a time,
to replicate its counterpart in the full precision space.
So you can run inputs through the full precision model.
You get the output, the probability is of each word, for example.
And then you replicate your quantized model
to reproduce, to get very close to those same weights.
And then you do the second layer.
So now you have the logits from the hidden states
from the second to last layer.
And then you train your quantized layer
to match those hidden states.
And you keep doing that all the way down.
And I think the intuition here is that by doing a layer
by layer distillation, you're sort of replicating
not just the output, which is kind of sparse and hard
to replicate, but even the flow of data
throughout the whole model step by step.
And you can replicate that into the quantized model,
which may run into issues when training just end to end.
And then the last work here, which Kram already talked
about two lectures ago, is Kulora.
So here, they use parameter-efficient fine tuning
to train a highly quantized, like, four-bit model.
And they do a bunch of other fancy tricks.
And Kulora is like super popular right now.
So this is probably the, if you're
going to use the quantization method today,
this probably would be it.
OK, I think I'm going to move on to pruning now.
So any questions here?
OK, so pruning is pretty different than quantization.
In quantization, you were sort of like chipping away
at every parameter in your model.
Instead in pruning, you're like completely eliminating
some parameters and completely not changing everything else.
So a number of parameters set to zero
and the rest are completely unchanged.
And the most intuitive way to do this
is this idea that if you have a bunch of parameters,
some of them are probably close to zero, in which case,
they're not doing anything anyways.
So just make them completely set to zero.
That way, you can ignore those parameters effectively.
They effectively are not doing anything.
And it's as if they don't exist.
So in magnitude pruning, you set to zero,
some percentage of parameters that have the least magnitude.
And in machine translation, people
have seen that you can remove almost half the parameters
in a model and get almost zero change
in your downstream performance.
Which I think goes back to the earlier point
about overparaporation.
Like you need a lot of these parameters
for training the model.
But in practice, they're not really doing too much.
And so you can just get rid of them.
And so this is a type of unstructured pruning
where you're just removing parameters throughout the model
anywhere you see fit.
There's no structure to how you're doing the pruning.
And this is related to the lottery ticket hypothesis,
which was this idea that when you train a full model,
there are randomly initially,
there are subnet works of that model that,
the idea is that when you're training a big model,
there are subnet works that are actually a better initialization
than the initial model.
So it doesn't, it's a little bit unintuitive,
that if you have, let's say, a model with 100,
with 100 billion parameters,
there's subnet works of this model
with even if you randomly initialize them,
that might say have a bigger,
but that's not the only way to do that.
So it's not the only way to do that.
If you randomly initialize them,
that might say have a billion parameters,
like 1% of the size, that are actually better
than the full model.
And so this is related to pruning,
but here they prune the model and they retrain it,
and they find that surprisingly,
which here a model is 20% the size.
So it's pruned to 20% of the original models parameters,
and then retrained is actually more effective
and generalizes better than your original model.
So the idea here is basically finding like,
really good initializations of these subnet works
can be better than like the most intuitive random
and misalization of a big model.
And so this is sort of a step beyond pruning,
where you're pruning a model,
then training on top of that,
and that can improve performance.
But generally pruning, I think,
is not a method to improve performance,
it's a method to maintain performance
while improving the efficiency and the size of your model.
And so there's been like a lot of cool work in pruning,
coming out of CMU recently.
This paper called Wanda, which came from folks in MLD.
The idea here is that magnitude pruning presumes
that you can just decide which parameters
you wanna throw away based on how big they are.
But it doesn't consider the fact that
there are systematic differences in the size of inputs
that come in.
So in the paper, they gave a nice example,
which maybe I'll write on the whiteboard here,
which is that.
